coredata.dc:title: Visible light degradation of tetracycline using oxygen-rich titanium dioxide nanosheets decorated by carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinghao
Yang, Ying
Li, Haipu
Yang, Zhaoguang
Fang, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) is widely applied as a broad-spectrum bacteriostat and has been detected in various water environments with its metabolites, thus posing a serious threat to the aquatic ecosystem and human health. In this work, a novel composite of carbon quantum dot oxygen-rich titanium dioxide nanosheets (CQD-OTNs) was successfully constructed and prepared by an improved two-step hydrothermal method and was applied in the photo-degradation of TC. The CQD-OTNs could increase the degradation ratio of TC to 94.1% with 120 min visible light irradiation, and the degradation rate was about 2.3 times higher than that of pure titanium dioxide nanosheets. The improved photocatalytic activity of CQD-OTNs was attributed to the constructed oxygen enriched structure and CQDs, by widening the longest absorption wavelength (from 375 of TNs to 420 nm), facilitating the separation of generated electron-holes (e--h+), and suppressing the recombination of the electron-hole pairs. The generated holes, hydroxyl radical (•OH), and superoxide radical (•O2 –) participated in the catalytic reaction, and the h+ dominated the degradation of TC. The toxicity of the degradation solution increased firstly in 10 min and then gradually decreased to that as ultrapure water. Furthermore, the possible degradation pathways of TC were proposed and discussed.

head.ce:title: Visible light degradation of tetracycline using oxygen-rich titanium dioxide nanosheets decorated by carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) is widely applied as a broad-spectrum bacteriostat and has been detected in various water environments with its metabolites, thus posing a serious threat to the aquatic ecosystem and human health. In this work, a novel composite of carbon quantum dot oxygen-rich titanium dioxide nanosheets (CQD-OTNs) was successfully constructed and prepared by an improved two-step hydrothermal method and was applied in the photo-degradation of TC. The CQD-OTNs could increase the degradation ratio of TC to 94.1% with 120 min visible light irradiation, and the degradation rate was about 2.3 times higher than that of pure titanium dioxide nanosheets. The improved photocatalytic activity of CQD-OTNs was attributed to the constructed oxygen enriched structure and CQDs, by widening the longest absorption wavelength (from 375 of TNs to 420 nm), facilitating the separation of generated electron-holes (e--h+), and suppressing the recombination of the electron-hole pairs. The generated holes, hydroxyl radical (•OH), and superoxide radical (•O2 –) participated in the catalytic reaction, and the h+ dominated the degradation of TC. The toxicity of the degradation solution increased firstly in 10 min and then gradually decreased to that as ultrapure water. Furthermore, the possible degradation pathways of TC were proposed and discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: According to the investigation and survey from European Union (EU) [1,2], about 3,000 kinds of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) are used for medicines (such as painkillers and antibiotics). In this respect, antibiotics, representing a significant class of PPCPs, play a key role in various fields [2–4].

Among them, tetracyclic antibiotics are mainly used in aquaculture for disease treatment around the world. However, they cannot be utilized very fully, and approximately 90% of the parents or metabolites are released into the environment. Nowadays, tetracyclic antibiotics have been detected in various water environments (such as groundwater, surface water, and so on) with the average concentration greater than 17 ng/L [5–7], and some rivers in China, tetracycline concentration even reached to 497 ng/L [8–10], which would bring about a series of problems, such as drug-resistant bacteria, food-chain enrichment, and chronic toxic effects [4], and thus pose a serious threat to human health and environmental safety. Considering the on-going massive production and consumption of tetracyclic antibiotics, it is eager to find an effective method and strategy for aqueous antibiotics elimination.

Advanced oxidation technologies (AOTs) including photochemical oxidation, ozone oxidation and Fenton oxidation, have been proved to be effective in the degradation of persistent organic pollutants, especially for antibiotics which are hard to be biodegraded due to their toxicities to microorganisms [11]. In recent years, the photo-catalysis oxidation utilizing semiconductors as catalysts has attracted widespread attention for the degradation of organic and inorganic pollutants [12,13]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2), a representative photocatalytic material, has been extensively researched [14,15], because it possesses unique advantages such as photocatalytic activity, low-cost, bulk availability, non-toxicity, and stability [16,17]. When TiO2 is exposed to ultraviolet light or sunlight, photons with energy higher than its band gap energy are absorbed, resulting in the promotion of electrons (e−) from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) and the formation of positively charged vacancies (h+, holes) in the VB. The generated e− and h+ move to the surface of the TiO2 and join in oxidation (h+) and reduction (e−) reactions to generate the active free radicals. The h+ possesses enough potential to oxidize water or OH– generating •OH radicals and the generated e− can react with O2 to produce superoxide anions •O2 – [18]. The produced •O2 – and •OH can participate in redox reactions to remove target pollutants.

TiO2-based photocatalytic materials have been used for air and water purification; however, only a few of them are practically applied [19]. This is mainly because TiO2 as the photo-catalyst has many limitations. Firstly, it has a relatively wide band gap (3.2 eV for anatase) compared with other materials, which needs to absorb ultraviolet light of wavelength less than 387.5 nm to meet the forbidden band energy; nevertheless, the sunlight only comprises 3–5% ultraviolet light and the abundant visible light (42–45%) cannot be used [20]. Secondly, its electron-hole pairs are easy to recombine because of the kinetically slow holes consuming reaction [21]. These disadvantages lead to reduced utilization of sunlight as well as the photocatalytic activity. Therefore it is very important to overcome these drawbacks by modifying TiO2.

In general, there are two strategies to modify TiO2 to improve its photocatalytic activity, namely, tuning the structure or the composition. On the one hand, the activity of TiO2 greatly depends on its phase structure, crystallinity, or morphology. For example, TiO2 nanosheet (TN) with lots of (001) facets possessed better photocatalytic activity than the regular TiO2 or P25 (a mixture of 80% anatase TiO2 and 20% rutile TiO2), because the (001) facets are more reactive than other facets [22]; since the nanosheet structure can effectively suppress the recombination of electron-holes, the oxygen-rich TiO2 was proved to possess better activity than P25 [23,24]. However, the photocatalytic activity of bare oxygen-rich TiO2 was decreased with increasing the duration of usage. On the other hand, TiO2 can be combined with other materials (including kinds of nonmetal (N, C, S, etc.), noble metals (Ag, Au, Pt, etc.) and narrow band gap semiconductors) by doping and deposition [16,25–27]. For examples, the different doses of N (0%, 0.2%, 0.5%, 1%, and 2%) was doped in the TiO2, and the optimal photocatalytic degradation performance of the composites was achieved at 1.0% of N doping [28]. The study further indicated that N doping caused oxygen vacancies, which restrained the electron-hole recombination and thus improved the photocatalytic performance [29], while too much N doping might form excessive oxygen vacancies and reduce the photocatalytic activity by facilitating the electron-hole pairs recombination [30]. Hajjaji et al. used different doses of Cr (0%, 2%, 4%, 7%, 8%, 13%, and 18%) to modify TiO2 nanoparticles hoping to improve the photocatalytic activity; however, the best photocatalytic performance for the degradation of pollutants was obtained for a Cr doping of 2%, which was due to the increased coverage of metal on the semiconductor surface and the decreased surface adsorption sites available to pollutants. And the overdoping could lead to the formation of secondary impurities [31]. Significantly, some researchers had successfully extended the photo-response into visible range and promoted the separation of photo-induced electron-hole pairs by combining the carbon materials (active carbon, carbon nanotubes, carbon membrane, graphene, etc.) with TiO2 [26,27,32]. The TiO2 particles were spread over the surface of carbon materials, and the photocatalytic activity of composite materials was improved compared with the pure TiO2. But the way to prepare materials might cause the aggregation of TiO2 particles and thus damage the photocatalytic activity [33]. Recently, a new typical functional carbon material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), is found possessing various excellent characteristics, such as luminescence, down-conversion and good up-converting photoluminescence and low cost, and has been widely used in fields of bio-imaging, bio-sensing, and especially photo-catalysis [34,35]. Li et al. [36] located CQDs on TiO2 nanosheets for photo-reduction of Cr(VI) under sunlight irradiation, and found that the recombination of electron-hole pairs was hindered and the photo-reduction activity of CQD-TN was promoted, which posed a light to us: the combination of the oxygen enriched TiO2 nanosheets (OTNs) with CQDs could result in a better photo-catalyst since the OTNs possess oxygen-rich functional groups and better photocatalytic activity than the regular TiO2.

In the present study, the composite of CQDs modified oxygen-rich TiO2 nanosheets (CQD-OTNs) was constructed by decorating CQDs onto OTNs through two-steps hydrothermal method and was used to degrade the antibiotics. The morphology, phase, chemical composition, and optical properties of CQD-OTNs were characterized; the process of photocatalytic degradation was optimized; importantly, the catalytic mechanism for the outstanding performance on TC degradation by the CQD-OTNs was studied; the intermediate products and degradation pathway of TC were proposed, and the biological toxicities of intermediate products were evaluated by inhibition zone method.

TC (purity: 99.99%) was purchased from National Institute for Drug Control (Beijing China). Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.9%) and tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide, (H2O2, 30.0%), ammonia (NH3·H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), humic acid (HA), tert-butanol (TBA), ammonium oxalate (AO), and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. HPLC grade methanol was obtained from Tedia Co. Ltd, America. And all the other chemical reagents were analytically pure and used without further purification. Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ·cm, 25 °C, UP water) was used in all experiments.
The process of preparing materials was shown in Scheme 1 .

OTNs were synthesized with fluorine induced by the hydrothermal method [37]. Briefly, 50 mL TBOT, 6 mL HF, and 20 mL H2O2 were put into the 200 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) stainless steel reactor, and the reactor was kept at 180 °C for 24 h in the oven. The obtained solids were washed with the NaOH aqueous solution to remove fluorinion (F-) and then washed by UP water until the pH value of the supernatant got to approximately 7. Finally, the produced solid particles were dried in the oven at 60 °C.

The pH value of the citric acid aqueous solution (0.2 mol/L) was adjusted to 6 by adding NH3·H2O. And 0.4 g OTNs was put into the solution. After stirring and ultrasound treatment for 30 min, the suspension was transferred to a PTFE stainless steel reactor and maintained at 160 °C for 4 h in the oven. The precipitates were separated by centrifugation and washed by UP water and dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

CQDs were prepared by the same method as §2.2.2 without adding OTNs. The particulates were removed by high-speed centrifugal force, and the obtained supernatant including CQDs was dried with a vacuum freeze-dryer overnight to get CQDs.

The CQD-OTNs were characterized by the morphology, phase, chemical composition, and photo-electric property. The phase of prepared materials was detected by the Bruker D8 Discover X-ray diffractometer using Ni-filtered Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm) at a scan rate of 5°/s. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) experiment was acquired by ATR accessories (Nicolet X700). X-ray photoelectron spectrums (XPS) were obtained on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI with Al-Kα radiation. The nanostructure was examined by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F microscope). UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was recorded with a CARY 300/PE lambda 750S. Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was measured on FLS9, Edinburgh fluorescence spectrophotometer. The electrochemical performance of materials of TNs, OTNs, CQD-TNs, and CQD-OTNs was determined by the three-electrode system on the CHI604 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., LTD). The fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO, 10 mm × 20 mm) coated glass covered with photo-catalyst was served as the working electrode. And the platinum sheet and Ag/AgCl were served as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (ESR) signals were monitored on a Bruker ER 200-SRC spectrometer under the irradiation of visible light. The total organic carbon (TOC) was detected by the TOC detector (Shimadzu, TOC-VL) to analyze the mineralization degree of contaminants.
Fig. S1 shows the diagram of the photocatalytic degradation device including the light source (500 W, Xenon arc lamp), quartz glass reactor, water-cooled circulating system, and magnetic stirring.

To explore the photocatalytic performance of CQD-OTNs, the 0.0625 g of catalyst was added into 125 mL of TC solution (15 mg/L). Before visible light irradiation, the pH was adjusted by 0.1 mol/L HCl or NaOH and then the suspension was stirred in the dark for 60 min to ensure the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. After that, the light was turned on and the photocatalytic degradation was initiated. At a preset time in 120 min, 1 mL sample was taken out and filtered by the 0.22 μm PTFE syringe filter to remove the solid powders for concentration determination.

As for ESR, a spin-trapping agent of 50 mmol/L 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used to detect the reactive species generated in the photocatalytic process. Furthermore, the used materials were washed three times with UP-water and then dried at 60 °C for a recycling experiment.

The TC concentration is detected by HPLC (Agilent 1260, USA) equipped with a DAD detector and the Agilent C-18 column (250 × 4.6 mm, 5 μm). The liquid phase conditions are water/methanol mixtures (1:1, v:v) as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1 mL/min and column temperature 30 °C [38]. The degradation intermediates are identified by HPLC-MS/MS (Agilent HPLC-6400, USA). The mobile phase is methanol/0.1% formic acid (50/50, v/v) at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The intermediates are distinguished in the positive ion mode using an electron–ion source (EIS) under the following conditions: m/z range of 20–800.

The Ct/C0 and η are used to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of prepared material for degradation TC. The value of η is calculated with the following equation (Eq. (1)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The bacteriostatic activity of undegraded TC or intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: The bacteriostatic activities of undegraded TC or intermediates were tested by the staphylococcus aureus ATCC25923. The solution (10 μL) including undegraded TC or intermediate products was dripped onto the drug-sensitive paper. After the papers were air-dried, the process was repeated to get the test paper for drug susceptibility tests. The drug-carrying papers were placed on the surface of solid LB medium, which had been inoculated by staphylococcus aureus ATCC25923. At last, the sizes of bacteriostatic zones were measured after incubation at 37 °C for 16 h. The detailed process of the bacterial activation test was provided in the Supplementary material, Text S1.

The crystalline structure of as-prepared materials was determined by XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 a, all four materials possessed good crystallinity. The peaks of prepared materials were in accordance with PDF No. 21–1272, and the three typical peaks located at 25.4°, 48.2°, and 55.0°, which were in agree with (101), (200), and (201) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively [39,40]. The results proved that TiO2 existed in the prepared materials. The (004) plane of prepared TNs was more broadened than the standard pattern, and the sharp (200) diffraction peak demonstrated that the crystal size along the (100)/(010) directions was quite large, indicating that the prepared TNs might have a highly exposed (001) facets. The exposed (001) facets could expand the optical response range to improve photocatalytic activity. As for the samples of OTNs and CQD-OTNs, the lattice parameters (a = 3.7936 Å, c = 9.4083 Å) had increased slightly compared with those of the parent TiO2 (a = 3.7748 Å, c = 9.3736 Å), and the c/a ratio decreased, implying the increased oxygen content in the modified TiO2 and the existence of oxygen enriched TiO2 (OTNs) [23].

As expected, there was no characteristic peak for CQDs in the XRD spectrum due to the amorphousness of CQDs in the study. However, In FT-IR characterization (Fig. 1b), all materials possessed a strong absorption band located at about 500–900 cm−1, which were in accordance with the vibration of Ti-O-Ti and O-Ti bonds, indicating that the existence of TiO2. Compared with the peaks of parent TiO2, a new shoulder peak ranging from 891 cm−1 to 905 cm−1, which was attributed to the vibration of O-O, appeared for the samples of OTNs and CQD-OTNs, implying the existence of oxygen enriched TiO2. The vibration of O-O was consistent with that reported by Tan et al [24]. The peaks at 1630 and 3250–3500 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching of the hydroxyl group of the adsorbed water [41,42]. The samples of TNs and OTNs had an obvious peak near the 1397 cm−1, and after the samples were modified with CQDs, the position of the peak was bathochromicaly shifted, which could be caused by the generation of C-O-Ti, suggesting the existence of C material and its interaction to TiO2 [43].

The structure and morphology of samples of CQDs and CQD-OTNs were investigated by HRTEM. The lattice fringe spacing of materials was determined with the software of Nano Measurer 1.2. As shown in Fig. 2 , the sample of CQD-OTNs presented a sheet-like shape and the lattice fringe spacing was about 0.351 nm, which corresponded to the (101) crystal planes of the TiO2 with rich oxygen [44,45]. Combined with the results of XRD and FT-IR, we could reasonably conclude that the sheet-like materials should be OTNs. It was also noticed that some particles were dispersed on the surface of OTNs. Based on the pre-design and FT-IR analysis, the particles might be CQDs. The pure CQDs prepared under the same condition without OTNs in the system have been demonstrated in Fig. 2a, and they were well dispersed with the average size of 4.58 nm [46] (Fig. 2b). In view of the almost same preparation condition, we speculated that CQDs were contained in the sample of CQD-OTNs, and the sample was the targeted material.

The XPS spectra showed the composition and chemical oxidation states of the CQD-OTNs sample (Fig. 3 ). The full survey spectrum included elements of C, O, Ti, and F. Among them, C 1 s, O 1 s, and Ti 2p were characterized by binding energies of 284.8, 458.5, and 537.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 3a). The element of F might come from residual HF (the material was not completely cleaned with 0.1 mol/L NaOH during the purification process). As shown in Fig. 3b, the C 1 s was de-convoluted into four peaks of 284.7, 285.6, 286.5, and 288.8 eV, assignable to C–C or C = C, C–H, C-O, and C = O bonds, respectively [47,48]. For the C 1 s from the sample of OTNs (Fig. S2), there were no peaks of C-O and C = O observed. This difference suggested the presence of CQDs in the TiO2 nanosheets [12]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3c) could be folded into three peaks at 529.8, 531.3, and 532.5 eV, which were assigned to Ti-O, O–H, and C-O bonds, respectively, indicating that the chemical interaction between CQDs and OTNs was in the form of Ti-O-C linkage, which was consistent with the FT-IR analysis [35,49]. Moreover, the peaks at 458.5 and 464.2 eV in the spectrum of Ti 2p were assigned to Ti 2p 3/2 and Ti 2p 1/2, respectively (Fig. 3d) [50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic dynamics of the prepared materials
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption and photocatalytic performance of TNs, OTNs, CQD-TNs, and CQD-OTNs were evaluated for removing TC (15 mg/L) from the aqueous solution, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 a. In order to investigate the reaction rate, the pseudo-first-order kinetic was used (Fig. 4b) and the reaction equilibrium was followed Eq (3):
(3)
- ln
C t
C 0
=
k t

where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the TC concentrations at initial time and time t (min), respectively, and k (min−1) is the apparent reaction rate constant. It is observed that there was no obvious removal of TC under the 120 min visible light irradiation without catalysts, while the photo-catalysts of TNs, OTNs, CQD-TNs, and CQD-OTNs could remove 54.2%, 60.6%, 74.5%, and 94.1% TC, respectively. CQD-OTNs exhibited the best photocatalytic performance for TC degradation, and the corresponding reaction rate (k = 0.0335 min−1) was about 2.3 times higher than that of TNs (k = 0.0101 min−1). The TNs possessed photocatalytic activity for TC degradation under visible light irradiation, which was caused by the highly active (001) facets. The result was consistent with XRD analysis.

As shown in Fig. 4c, with increasing catalyst dosage, the photocatalytic performance increased for TC degradation. In the absence of CQD-OTNs (i.e. the concentration was 0 mg/L), the TC degradation ratio was only 4.2% with the 120 min visible light irradiation. After adding CQD-OTNs with the dosages of 300, 500, and 800 mg/L, the degradation ratios of TC increased to 74.9%, 85.0%, and 100%, respectively, under the 60 min visible light irradiation. The higher catalyst dosage did not give an obvious increase in photocatalytic performance for TC degradation. However, further increasing the irradiation time to 120 min improved the degradation ratios in all systems to over 90%. As known, the photocatalytic activity was closely related to the number of reactive sites on photo-catalyst and the penetration of visible light irradiation. A low dose of photo-catalyst and insufficient reactive sites for target pollutions led to low photocatalytic performance [51]; while a too high dose of photo-catalyst would level off photocatalytic performance for pollutions degradation due to the shielding effect and light reflection [52].

As a representative natural organic matter generally existing in the water environment, HA possessed a large number of functional groups such as carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl, which could react with the surface groups of photo-catalysts directly or indirectly [53]. As shown in Fig. 4d, the photocatalytic performance of CQD-OTNs for TC degradation decreased with increasing the concentration of HA. The photo-degradation ratio after 120 min irradiation of visible light gradually decreased from initial 93.3% (0 mg/L HA) to 72.9% (30 mg/L HA), while the reaction kinetics constants decreased from 0.0335 min−1 to 0.0151 min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of CQD-OTNs with other CQDs-based photo-catalysts in photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: An overview of published works on TC degradation based on the CQDs-based photo-catalysts as well as ours in this work was shown in Table 1 . Among the reported materials, the CQDs@MIL-125(Ti) with the metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) as emerging inorganic–organic hybrid materials possessed an effective degradation of TC (degradation ratio of 90%) in 240 min visible light irradiation. The degradation ratios of TC using CQDs/Bi2WO6, CQDs/g-C3N4, CQDs/Bi2MoO6 were about 78% (120 min), 78.6% (240 min), and 63% (120 min), respectively. When compared with these pioneer work, the title CQD-OTNs, obtained by simple preparation process, showed a relatedly high competitiveness in efficiency (94.1%, 120 min) and cost, and might be potentially practically used in environmental purification.

The photocatalytic performance of the material is closely related to the use efficiency of light, the photo-generated electron-holes separation efficiency, and the recombination capacity of photo-generated electron-holes. The UV–vis spectra were used to investigate the efficiency of the use of light. As shown in Fig. 5 a, TNs could absorb the lights of wavelength below 375 nm. In contrast, OTNs widened the longest absorption wavelength to 400 nm, while CQD-TNs to 405 nm, and CQD-OTNs further to 420 nm. The results confirmed that both CQDs and the oxygen enriched structure were able to extend the light absorption range from ultraviolet to visible light, facilitating the generation of more electron-hole pairs [58]. The PL spectra were used to explore the recombination capacity of photo-generated electron-holes by following the rule of the positive correlation of the intensity and the capacity [59]. As shown in Fig. 5b, the PL intensity of CQD-OTNs was the lowest, indicating the effective promotion of the separation of photo-generated electron-holes pairs by CQDs and the oxygen enriched structure [58]. EIS was employed to analyze the charge carrier separation efficiency [60,61]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the diameter of the arc radius on the Nyquist plots of the CQD-OTNs was smaller than that of the TNs, OTNs, and CQD-TNs in order, indicating a small charge transfer resistance of CQD-OTNs, which could be due to the promoted photo-generated electron transfer from OTNs or TNs, facilitated by CQDs and the oxygen enriched structure [36]. According to the above results of UV–vis, EIS, and PL analyses, it can be drawn that, CQDs and the oxygen enriched structure not only enhance the absorption of light but also promote the separation efficiency of electron-holes and effectively restrain electron-hole recombination.

To further explore the generated reactive species in the system of CQD-OTNs with the visible light, the ESR technique was applied. DMPO reagent was generally used for the detection of •O2 – in methanol and •OH in UP-water, respectively [62]. As shown in Fig. 5d and 5e, there were no obvious signals of •O2 – and •OH in the dark condition, while the noticeable signals of •O2 – and •OH came out in the spectra after 10 min visible light irradiation, indicating that the •O2 – and •OH existed in the photocatalytic system and they were generated by the exposure of light to CQD-OTNs.

In order to investigate the contributions of various reactive species in the TC photocatalytic process with CQD-OTNs, the free radical trap experiment was carried out. The inhibitor of IPA (•OH inhibitors), BQ (•O2 – inhibitors) or AO (h+ inhibitors) was added into the reaction solution. The inhibition ratio of the inhibitors for TC degradation in the free radical trap experiment was calculated with Eq. (4).
(4)
Inhibition

As shown in Fig. 5f, the photocatalytic system without inhibitors degraded 94.1% of TC with 120 min visible light irradiation. After adding IPA, BQ, or OA, the degradation ratios of TC were reduced to 82.2%, 59.6%, and 28%, respectively. IPA had the minimum inhibitory effect with an inhibition ratio of 11.9%, while BQ and OA possessed obvious inhibitory effects with inhibition ratio of 34.5% and 66.1%, respectively, implying that the h+ and •O2 – played important roles in the photocatalytic degradation of TC [63]. The results could explain the inhibition effect of HA in the degradation of TC as following: HA not only consumed a large amount of h+ to produce HA+ that could react with photo-electrons but also could quench the •O2 – and •OH in the long-term photocatalytic reaction [64]. The mechanism could be expressed concisely with Eqs. (5) - (7) [64,65]:

According to the above results and scientific literature [36,66,67], the photocatalytic mechanism for CQD-OTNs was proposed, as shown in Fig. 6 . The CQD-OTNs were excited by visible light to generate electron-holes. Because of the confined space effect, the generated electrons migrated from the interior to the surface of the material to achieve the separation of electron-hole pairs. During the photocatalytic process, the CQDs transferred the long-wavelength light (greater than 440 nm) into the short-wavelength light (less than440 nm) range, to accept the photo-generated electrons from OTNs (as Eqs. (8) - (10)), which could effectively suppress the recombination of electron-holes [68]. Furthermore, the photo-generated electrons on the surface of CQDs and OTNs could react with O2 to generate •O2 – and the holes could react with H2O to produce •OH (as Eqs. (11) - (13)). The generated h+, •O2 – and •OH possessed strong oxidation capacity for degradation of TC in the solution or adsorbed on the surface of the material. The photocatalytic mechanism could be demonstrated as Eqs. (8) - (15) [47].

HPLC-MS/MS was used to distinguish the degradation products with the 90 min visible light irradiation with CQD-OTNs. As shown in Fig. S3, the obvious parent peak of TC appeared at the retention time of 6.04 min; and after the degradation for 90 min, many new peaks appeared, indicating that the TC could well be degraded by a photocatalytic system with CQD-OTNs. As listed in the Table. S1, the intermediate products were characterized using ESI (+)-MS.

Those generated reactive species could easily attract electron-rich functional groups (amino group, aromatic ring, and double bond) [69]. As shown in Fig. 7 , one possible pathway of TC degradation is that the TC molecule (m/z = 445) was transformed into the compound I, possibly due to the attack on the amino group (position of C4 in TC) by reactive species, and it was oxidized into a ketone group (I) [70]. Following this, compound IV appeared (m/z = 365) after the attack on double bonds C2 = C3 by •OH. Another pathway suggested that the formation of hydroxyl at C11a (TC) and ketone group at C12 (TC), to afford compound II [71]. After that, compound III was formed from the oxidation of compound II at the C4 position. The products with the larger molecule weight (compounds II, III, and IV) were broken into some small molecules in further treatment to form ring compounds V and VI [72,73], which could be oxidized to small organic molecules (compound VII and VIII) through the open-ring reactions [74]. At last, some of them were mineralized to CO2 and H2O. The TOC removal rate of the solution was shown in Fig. 8 a. With the increase of photo-catalysis time, the TOC decreased gradually from 50.74 mg/L to 37.28 mg/L after 120 min visible light irradiation.

The TOC removal ratio and the degradation ratio of TC were 26.52% and 94.1%, respectively (Fig. 8a), indicating that many intermediate products were generated in the process of photocatalytic degradation of TC. Given the possible more toxicity of intermediate products, the experiment of inhibition zone of Staphylococcus aureus ATCC25923 was used to estimate the toxicity of the treated solution [75]. As shown in Fig. 8b and Fig. S4, the diameter of the inhibition zone of the solution without treatment 15 mg/L TC (0 min) was approximately 9.89 mm. It increased firstly to 11.92 mm at 10 min, indicating the increasing toxicity of the intermediate products than that of TC. After that, the diameters of the bacteriostatic zone went down gradually to 6.42 mm at 120 min irradiation, which was comparable with that of blank water. This result suggested that some intermediate products were more toxic than TC, while the toxicity of final products could be almost ignored. In other words, the photo-catalysis process could be a promising candidate in the toxicity control of the aqueous solution with TC.

The photocatalytic performance of CQD-OTNs for degradation of TC in practical waters (tap water and river water) under visible light irradiation was further investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 9 . Compared with UP-water, the degradation ratios of TC were 89.9% in tap water and 52.8% in river water, respectively, suggesting that CQD-OTNs can be practically used for the deep treatment of tap water. Compared with UP-water, tap water contains Cl- and HCO3 – that could act as a free radical inhibitor and somewhat reduced the performance [76]. As for river water, the organic matter therein could compete with TC to react with reactive species [77].

The stability and reusability are important parameters for materials in practical application. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the degradation ratio of CQD-OTNs was still over 90% after four cycles. Fig. 10b showed the XRD patterns of the initial and used CQD-OTNs, suggesting that the original crystal phase was retained and the intensity even increased more or less after irradiation of visible light. The above results indicated that the CQD-OTNs possessed excellent stability in photocatalytic activity.

CQD-OTNs could be prepared by an improved two-step hydrothermal method and possessed excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of TC compared with TNs, OTNs, and CQD-TNs. After 120 min visible light irradiation, the toxicity of the final solution was insignificant. The UV–vis, PL, and EIS spectra indicated that the improved photocatalytic activity was attributed to CQDs and the oxygen enriched structure. In the photo-degradation, h+ played an important role, followed by the •O2 –, and •OH. The generated reactive species easily attacked electron-rich functional groups (amino group, aromatic ring, and double bond) for TC mineralization. Inorganic ions and natural organic matter in water could negatively influence the photocatalytic performance of CQD-OTNs for the degradation of TC. This study might provide some new inspiration for building the novel and efficient photo-catalysts, and prompting their practical application in environmental remediation.
